args_redacted.query: zero trust architecture NIST principles never trust always verify
defense in depth layered security strategy overview

args_redacted.raw:
{"path": "workspace/security_comparison.txt", "content": "================================================================================
  COMPARISON: ZERO TRUST SECURITY vs. DEFENSE IN DEPTH
================================================================================

1. OVERVIEW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST SECURITY
  A security model centered on the principle of "never trust, always verify."
  It assumes that threats exist both outside and inside the traditional network
  perimeter, and therefore no user, device, or system should be trusted by
  default — even if already inside the network. Every access request must be
  authenticated, authorized, and continuously validated.

DEFENSE IN DEPTH
  A layered security strategy that employs multiple, overlapping defensive
  controls to protect systems and data. The idea is that if one layer fails,
  the next layer continues to provide protection. It draws inspiration from
  military strategy, where multiple lines of defense slow and ultimately stop
  an attacker.

================================================================================

2. CORE PRINCIPLES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST PRINCIPLES:
  • Never Trust, Always Verify — No implicit trust based on network location.
  • Least Privilege Access — Users and systems get only the minimum access
    needed to perform their function, and only for the duration needed.
  • Micro-Segmentation — Networks are divided into small, isolated zones;
    moving between zones requires separate authentication and authorization.
  • Continuous Monitoring & Validation — Sessions and access rights are
    continuously inspected and re-validated; trust is never permanent.
  • Assume Breach — Operate as if the network is already compromised; design
    controls to limit blast radius and lateral movement.
  • Identity-Centric — Identity (user, device, application) is the new
    perimeter; strong multi-factor authentication (MFA) is essential.
  • Explicit Verification — Every access decision is based on multiple signals:
    identity, device health, location, data classification, and risk level.

DEFENSE IN DEPTH PRINCIPLES:
  • Layered Protection — Deploy multiple, independent security controls across
    physical, technical, and administrative domains.
  • No Single Point of Failure — Redundancy ensures that the failure of one
    control does not result in total compromise.
  • Diversity of Controls — Use different types of controls (preventive,
    detective, corrective, deterrent) from different vendors/technologies.
  • Perimeter-First Mindset — Traditionally starts with a strong outer boundary
    (firewalls, IDS/IPS) and builds inward.
  • Depth Across Domains — Layers span physical security (locks, guards),
    network security (firewalls, VLANs), host security (antivirus, EDR),
    application security (WAF, secure coding), and data security (encryption).
  • Administrative Controls — Policies, procedures, training, and awareness
    programs form a critical human layer.

================================================================================

3. KEY DIFFERENCES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

┌──────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────┐
│ ASPECT               │ ZERO TRUST                  │ DEFENSE IN DEPTH             │
├──────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────┤
│                      │                             │                              │
│ Core Philosophy      │ Trust is a vulnerability;   │ Layers compensate for the    │
│                      │ eliminate implicit trust    │ weaknesses of individual     │
│                      │ entirely.                   │ controls.                    │
├──────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────┤
│                      │                             │                              │
│ Network Assumption   │ The network is always       │ The network can be secured   │
│                      │ hostile; internal = external│ with enough layers; internal │
│                      │ in terms of trust.          │ is safer than external.      │
├──────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────┤
│                      │                             │                              │
│ Perimeter Concept    │ No traditional perimeter;   │ Strong perimeter is the      │
│                      │ identity IS the perimeter.  │ first and critical layer.    │
├──────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────┤
│                      │                             │                              │
│ Access Control       │ Dynamic, risk-based,        │ Often static, role-based,    │
│                      │ session-by-session.         │ with broader standing access.│
├──────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────┤
│                      │                             │

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST SECURITY

  an attacker.

================================================================================

2. CORE PRINCIPLES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST PRINCIPLES:
  • Never Trust, Always Verify — No implicit trust based on network location.
  • Least Privilege Access — Users and systems get only the minimum access
    needed to perform their function, and only for the duration needed.
  • Micro-Segmentation — Networks are divided into small, isolated zones;
    moving between zones requires separate authentication and authorization.
  • Continuous Monitoring & Validation — Sessions and access rights are
    continuously inspected and re-validated; trust is never permanent.
  • Assume Breach — Operate as if the network is already compromised; design
    controls to limit blast radius and lateral movement.
  • Identity-Centric — Identity (user, device, application) is the new
    perimeter; strong multi-factor authentication (MFA) is essential.
  • Explicit Verification — Every access decision is based on multiple signals:
    identity, device health, location, data classification, and risk level.

DEFENSE IN DEPTH PRINCIPLES:
  • Layered Protection — Deploy multiple, independent security controls across
    physical, technical, and administrative domains.
  • No Single Point of Failure — Redundancy ensures that the failure of one
    control does not result in total compromise.
  • Diversity of Controls — Use different types of controls (preventive,
    detective, corrective, deterrent) from different vendors/technologies.
  • Perimeter-First Mindset — Traditionally starts with a strong outer boundary
    (firewalls, IDS/IPS) and builds inward.
  • Depth Across Domains — Layers span physical security (locks, guards),
    network security (firewalls, VLANs), host security (antivirus, EDR),
    application security (WAF, secure coding), and data security (encryption).
  • Administrative Controls — Policies, procedures, training, and awareness
    programs form a critical human layer.

================================================================================

3. KEY DIFFERENCES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ASPECT               | ZERO TRUST                          | DEFENSE IN DEPTH
---------------------|-------------------------------------|------------------------------------
Core Philosophy      | Trust is a vulnerability;           | Layers compensate for the
                     | eliminate implicit trust entirely.  | weaknesses of individual controls.
---------------------|-------------------------------------|------------------------------------
Network Assumption   | The network is always hostile;      | The network can be secured with
                     | internal = external in trust.       | enough layers; internal is safer
                     |                                     | than external.
---------------------|-------------------------------------|------------------------------------
Perimeter Concept    | No traditional perimeter;           | Strong perimeter is the first
                     | identity IS the perimeter.          | and critical layer.
---------------------|-------------------------------------|------------------------------------
Access Control       | Dynamic, risk-based,                | Often static, role-based, with
                     | session-by-session.                 | broader standing access.
---------------------|-------------------------------------|------------------------------------
Lateral Movement     | Actively blocked via micro-         | Slowed by layers but not
                     | segmentation; every hop requires    | inherently prevented; once inside
                     | re-authentication.                  | the perimeter, movement is easier.
---------------------|-------------------------------------|------------------------------------
Trust Model          | Trust is never granted implicitly;  | Trust is extended within the
                     | it is continuously earned and       | perimeter; internal traffic is
                     | verified.                           | generally trusted.
---------------------|-------------------------------------|------------------------------------
Primary Focus        | Identity, data, and sessions.       | Network, hosts, and applications.
---------------------|-------------------------------------|------------------------------------
Architecture         | Modern, cloud-native, designed      | Traditional, can be adapted to
